subtopic_detail: Monochorionic-diamniotic twin surveillance for TTTS and selective growth restriction
stem: You are the obstetric registrar in a tertiary maternal-fetal medicine clinic reviewing a 31-year-old woman who is 18+0 weeks by first-trimester crown-rump length in a spontaneously conceived twin pregnancy. The dating scan at 12 weeks showed two fetuses, a single placental mass, a thin intertwin membrane with a T-sign, and concordant nuchal translucency measurements. Her routine 18-week morphology scan today shows two structurally normal fetuses but the deepest vertical pocket of amniotic fluid in twin A is 1.4 cm and in twin B is 9.2 cm; estimated fetal weights are within 12% of each other and umbilical artery Dopplers are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 82, T 36.8 °C, fundal height 24 cm, no uterine activity
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, cervix long and closed on transvaginal ultrasound at 38 mm
data_table.Investigations: Routine antenatal bloods normal, 18-week scan as above, both fetuses with normal cardiac four-chamber view

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue routine antenatal care with the next ultrasound at 28 weeks because the discordance in fluid volumes is within physiological variation for monochorionic-diamniotic twin pregnancies at this gestation
options.rationale: The combination of DVP under 2 cm in one sac and over 8 cm in the other meets diagnostic criteria for twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome, which mandates urgent specialist review rather than routine follow-up. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of monochorionic twin pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of monochorionic twin pregnancy

options.text: Arrange amnioreduction of the polyhydramniotic sac as a temporising day-stay procedure and review again in two weeks to assess whether the deepest vertical pocket discordance has stabilised on serial transabdominal ultrasound scans
options.rationale: Amnioreduction is a temporising measure and is inferior to fetoscopic laser photocoagulation for Stage II TTTS before 26 weeks; referral for laser is the recommended definitive treatment. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of monochorionic twin pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of monochorionic twin pregnancy

options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg intramuscularly now with a second dose in 24 hours and plan delivery by caesarean section once a viable gestational age beyond 24+0 weeks has been reached
options.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids and delivery are not indicated for a stable 18-week pregnancy without fetal compromise, prelabour rupture of membranes or imminent preterm birth. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of monochorionic twin pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of monochorionic twin pregnancy

options.text: Refer urgently to a fetal medicine unit for fetoscopic laser ablation of placental anastomoses and continue fortnightly monochorionic surveillance with deepest vertical pocket, growth and middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity assessment
options.rationale: Quintero stage I TTTS with progressing discordance, or stage II once oligohydramnios is present (DVP under 2 cm) with polyhydramnios (DVP over 8 cm before 20 weeks), is referred to a fetoscopic laser centre; fortnightly DVP, growth and MCA-PSV monitoring continues. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of monochorionic twin pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of monochorionic twin pregnancy

options.text: Arrange amniocentesis of both sacs for karyotype and parvovirus B19 PCR because monochorionic twins with discordant amniotic fluid should be evaluated for chromosomal or infective causes of growth and fluid discordance
options.rationale: Monochorionic twins share the same karyotype and parvovirus is not a typical cause of isolated discordant fluid volumes; the picture is intrinsic to placental vascular anastomoses. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of monochorionic twin pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of monochorionic twin pregnancy

explanation.summary: Monochorionic-diamniotic twins share a placenta with vascular anastomoses, putting them at risk of twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome. The Quintero staging system uses deepest vertical pocket discordance (under 2 cm in one sac, over 8 cm in the other before 20 weeks or over 10 cm after) as the diagnostic criterion. Stage I has fluid discordance alone, stage II adds absent bladder visualisation in the donor, stages III to V add Doppler abnormality, hydrops and demise. Fetoscopic laser photocoagulation of anastomoses is the standard treatment for stages II and above before 26 weeks. RANZCOG recommends fortnightly DVP, growth and MCA-PSV scans from 16 weeks for all MCDA pregnancies.
key_points: MCDA twins: fortnightly DVP, growth and Doppler from 16 weeks
TTTS diagnosis: DVP under 2 cm one sac plus over 8 cm other before 20 weeks
Stage I = fluid discordance only; stage II = donor bladder not seen
Fetoscopic laser is treatment of choice for stage II or higher before 26 weeks
MCA-PSV over 1.5 MoM in either twin suggests TAPS and warrants referral
explanation.pearls: T-sign at the placental insertion (rather than lambda) confirms monochorionicity in the first trimester; this single scan finding redirects the entire antenatal surveillance pathway for the rest of the pregnancy.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Management of monochorionic twin pregnancy
ISUOG Practice Guidelines: role of ultrasound in twin pregnancy

subtopic_detail: History-indicated cervical cerclage timing in recurrent mid-trimester loss
stem: You are the antenatal clinic registrar seeing a 33-year-old G4P0 at 11+5 weeks. She has had three previous pregnancies ending in painless mid-trimester losses at 19, 20 and 22 weeks, each preceded by sudden membrane rupture without contractions or bleeding. There is no history of cervical surgery, infection or trauma. She is otherwise well, takes folic acid and iodine, and dating ultrasound confirms a singleton with normal nuchal translucency. She asks what can be done in this pregnancy to reduce the risk of another mid-trimester loss.

data_table.Examination: Cervix appears closed on speculum; transvaginal length 35 mm with no funnelling
data_table.Investigations: Full blood count, thyroid function and HbA1c normal; antiphospholipid screen negative on 12-week recheck after prior negative result

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intervention?

options.text: Commence vaginal progesterone 200 micrograms nightly from 16 weeks and arrange fortnightly transvaginal cervical length surveillance from 16 to 24 weeks, deferring any cerclage decision until cervical shortening below 25 mm is documented
options.rationale: Progesterone with surveillance is appropriate for a single prior preterm birth or short cervix, but three prior mid-trimester losses meet criteria for history-indicated rather than ultrasound-indicated cerclage. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical insufficiency and cerclage)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Cervical insufficiency and cerclage

options.text: Plan elective McDonald cervical cerclage at 12 to 14 weeks gestation under regional anaesthesia with single-dose perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis and removal at 36 to 37 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Three or more prior second-trimester losses with the classical painless dilatation pattern is the prototypical indication for history-indicated cerclage placed at 12 to 14 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical insufficiency and cerclage)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Cervical insufficiency and cerclage

options.text: Arrange transabdominal cerclage at 14 to 16 weeks gestation via open laparotomy because of the failure of three previous pregnancies, accepting that delivery will require elective caesarean section
options.rationale: Transabdominal cerclage is reserved for failed transvaginal cerclage or for women with prior trachelectomy or extensive cervical loss, not as first-line treatment. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical insufficiency and cerclage)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Cervical insufficiency and cerclage

options.text: Recommend strict bed rest from 16 weeks gestation onward with hospital admission for monitoring from 18 weeks gestation until 24 weeks gestation and use of an Arabin pessary if shortening develops
options.rationale: Bed rest is not effective in cervical insufficiency and has documented harms including venous thromboembolism, deconditioning and psychological distress. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical insufficiency and cerclage)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Cervical insufficiency and cerclage

options.text: Provide reassurance, schedule routine antenatal care and counsel that no specific intervention has proven benefit in this scenario given the absence of cervical shortening on her booking transvaginal scan
options.rationale: Doing nothing despite a clear history of cervical insufficiency abandons an intervention that reduces the risk of preterm delivery by around 50% in appropriately selected women. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical insufficiency and cerclage)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Cervical insufficiency and cerclage

explanation.summary: Cervical insufficiency is a clinical diagnosis based on a history of recurrent painless mid-trimester losses without infection, bleeding or contractions. RANZCOG recommends history-indicated cerclage at 12 to 14 weeks gestation in women with three or more prior second-trimester losses or preterm births before 28 weeks. Surveillance with transvaginal cervical length and ultrasound-indicated cerclage at length under 25 mm is reserved for those with one or two prior events. Cerclage is removed at 36 to 37 weeks or earlier in preterm labour. Outcomes are best when the procedure is performed before any cervical shortening develops.
key_points: History-indicated cerclage: 3+ prior 2nd-trimester losses or PTB before 28 weeks
Place at 12-14 weeks under regional anaesthesia
Ultrasound-indicated cerclage: cervical length under 25 mm before 24 weeks with 1-2 prior PTB
Remove cerclage at 36-37 weeks or in established preterm labour
Bed rest has no benefit and increases VTE risk
explanation.pearls: Classical cervical insufficiency presents with painless cervical dilatation in the late second trimester followed by membrane rupture and rapid expulsion; this is distinct from infection-driven preterm labour and requires a structural rather than tocolytic response.

subtopic_detail: Vacuum versus forceps choice in delayed second stage at full station with adequate analgesia
stem:
You are the obstetric registrar called to a primiparous woman at 40+3 weeks in established second stage of labour for 2 hours and 50 minutes despite oxytocin augmentation and active pushing for 90 minutes. She has a working epidural sited 6 hours ago with bilateral T10 block. The CTG shows late decelerations with reduced variability for the last 12 minutes.

The vertex is at +2 station, occiput-anterior with no caput, no moulding and the head is fully engaged on abdominal palpation. The bladder is empty after recent catheterisation.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 96, T 37.0 °C, fetal HR baseline 140 with late decelerations to 100
data_table.Examination: Vertex at +2, OA, no caput or moulding, fully dilated, membranes ruptured, clear liquor
data_table.Investigations: Fetal scalp lactate 4.1 mmol/L, maternal blood group O positive, antibody screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in delivery?

options.text: Continue active maternal pushing with directed coaching for a further 30 minutes and only escalate to instrumental delivery if the fetal heart rate trace deteriorates further or if no progress is made on the next vaginal examination
options.rationale: Late decelerations with a rising scalp lactate over 4 mmol/L meets criteria for expedited delivery; continued pushing is not appropriate. (Source: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth

options.text: Transfer urgently to theatre for category 1 caesarean section because instrumental delivery from +2 station carries an unacceptable risk of failed instrumental delivery and neonatal injury at this advanced maternal effort point
options.rationale: An OA vertex at +2 with full engagement, no caput and no moulding meets criteria for outlet instrumental birth; category 1 caesarean is more morbid and slower in this scenario. (Source: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth

options.text: Perform a midcavity rotational Kielland forceps delivery in the labour room with infiltration of the perineum using 1% lignocaine 10 mL prior to the application of the blades to the fetal head
options.rationale: Rotational forceps are not indicated for an already OA position and require theatre and trial-of-instrumental settings under regional anaesthesia, not labour-ward perineal infiltration. (Source: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth

options.text: Perform a Ventouse delivery with a Kiwi single-use cup using sequential pulls with contractions only, accepting up to three cup detachments before considering escalation to forceps or to theatre for trial of instrumental delivery
options.rationale: More than two pop-offs is a failed vacuum and represents an indication to stop and reassess, not to continue to three detachments. (Source: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth

options.text: Perform an outlet Ventouse delivery with a soft cup in the labour room using maternal effort with each contraction and a maximum of three pulls and two cup detachments before recourse to forceps or theatre
options.rationale: OA vertex at +2 with no caput or moulding, working epidural and full bladder empty meets criteria for outlet vacuum in the labour room with the recognised three-pull, two-detachment stop rule. (Source: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth

explanation.summary: Operative vaginal birth choice considers station, position, caput, moulding, pelvic capacity and operator skill. Outlet vacuum is suitable when the head is on the perineum or OA at low station with no significant caput or moulding. Vacuum has lower maternal genital trauma but more cephalhaematoma and retinal haemorrhage; forceps has higher perineal trauma but lower failure. The three-pull, two-detachment, 20-minute rule is widely used; failure mandates escalation. Trial of instrumental in theatre is reserved for midcavity or rotational deliveries with potential for caesarean section.
key_points: Outlet vacuum criteria: OA vertex, +2 or lower, no caput or moulding, bladder empty
Vacuum stop rule: 3 pulls, 2 pop-offs, 20 minutes - do not exceed
Forceps preferred over vacuum if maternal effort poor or fetus preterm under 34 weeks
Trial of instrumental in theatre for midcavity or rotational
Document indication, position, station, instrument, pulls and complications
explanation.pearls: If the head is not visible at the introitus between contractions and the sagittal suture is not in the AP diameter, treat as midcavity and move to theatre - underestimating station outside theatre is a recurring root cause of bad outcomes in coronial reviews.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline 26: Assisted vaginal birth

subtopic_detail: External cephalic version offer at term for uncomplicated breech presentation
stem: You are the antenatal clinic registrar reviewing a 28-year-old G2P1 at 36+5 weeks in an uncomplicated singleton pregnancy. The first pregnancy was a normal vaginal delivery of a 3400 g infant. Examination today suggests a longitudinal lie with breech presentation, confirmed on bedside ultrasound as a frank breech with normal amniotic fluid, posterior placenta, estimated fetal weight 2900 g and reassuring umbilical artery Dopplers. She has no contraindications to vaginal delivery and is asking about her options for the rest of the pregnancy and delivery.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/70 mmHg, HR 78, T 36.7 °C, fundal height 34 cm, audible fetal heart 142
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus, frank breech with head palpable in fundus, cervix not assessed; no uterine activity
data_table.Investigations: 36-week ultrasound as above, group B Strep swab pending, blood group A positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Recommend elective caesarean section at 39 weeks gestation now without further attempts at version because the Term Breech Trial established lower neonatal morbidity with planned caesarean for term breech presentation
options.rationale: Recommending caesarean without first offering ECV omits an evidence-based intervention that achieves cephalic version in around 50% of attempts and reduces caesarean rates. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of breech presentation at term)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of breech presentation at term

options.text: Encourage daily moxibustion at the BL67 acupoint and knee-chest positioning exercises for 10 minutes three times daily and review at 38 weeks to reassess fetal presentation
options.rationale: Postural and complementary measures do not have robust evidence of effect on persistent breech presentation at term in RANZCOG-aligned guidance. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of breech presentation at term)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of breech presentation at term

options.text: Offer external cephalic version at 37+0 weeks in a unit with theatre and CTG facilities with tocolysis, anti-D if Rh negative, and counselling on a 50% success rate and rare risks of placental abruption or emergency caesarean
options.rationale: RANZCOG recommends offering ECV from 37+0 weeks in uncomplicated breech with appropriate facilities; success around 40 to 60% and reduces caesarean for breech significantly. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of breech presentation at term)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of breech presentation at term

options.text: Recommend planned vaginal breech birth at the local district hospital with continuous CTG monitoring without first attempting external cephalic version because of her favourable obstetric history of one prior normal vaginal birth
options.rationale: Planned vaginal breech requires senior obstetric and neonatal expertise and is offered after counselling, not as a first step that bypasses ECV. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of breech presentation at term)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of breech presentation at term

options.text: Arrange weekly transvaginal cervical length scans from 37 weeks and admit for induction of labour with prostaglandin pessary at 38 weeks if cervical length is favourable for breech vaginal delivery
options.rationale: Induction of labour with a breech presentation is not standard practice and prostaglandin induction with breech presentation is not recommended in Australian guidance. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of breech presentation at term)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of breech presentation at term

explanation.summary: Around 3% of singleton pregnancies present breech at term. RANZCOG recommends offering external cephalic version from 37+0 weeks in uncomplicated cases. Success rates are 40 to 60% and higher in multiparous women, posterior placenta, normal amniotic fluid and complete or footling breech. Contraindications include placenta praevia, abnormal CTG, ruptured membranes, recent antepartum haemorrhage, multiple pregnancy and prior classical caesarean. Tocolysis with terbutaline or salbutamol improves success; anti-D is given to Rh-negative women. If ECV fails or is declined, planned caesarean section reduces perinatal mortality compared with planned vaginal breech (Term Breech Trial).
key_points: Offer ECV from 37+0 weeks in uncomplicated breech
Success 40-60%; higher in multips, normal AFI, posterior placenta
Tocolysis (salbutamol or terbutaline) improves success
Anti-D 625 IU if Rh negative; CTG before and after
Planned caesarean reduces perinatal mortality vs vaginal breech (Term Breech Trial)
explanation.pearls: An anterior placenta is not a contraindication to ECV but reduces success by about 15%; offering ECV remains worthwhile because the only alternatives are caesarean section or planned vaginal breech, each with their own counselling burden.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Management of breech presentation at term
Cochrane review: External cephalic version for breech presentation at term

subtopic_detail: VBAC eligibility and risks counselling with prior lower-segment caesarean
stem:
You are the obstetric registrar in clinic seeing a 32-year-old G3P2 at 30+0 weeks who is asking about a trial of vaginal birth after caesarean. Her first delivery was an uncomplicated normal vaginal birth at 39 weeks. Her second was an elective caesarean at 39+4 weeks for breech presentation; the operation note documents a low transverse uterine incision with no extension and an uncomplicated postoperative course.

The current pregnancy is uncomplicated, the fetus is cephalic with estimated weight on the 50th centile, and she lives 25 minutes from a maternity unit with 24-hour theatre, anaesthesia and on-site paediatric services.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/68 mmHg, HR 80, BMI 27, fundal height 30 cm
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus, cephalic presentation, regular fetal heart rate 148
data_table.Investigations: Routine antenatal bloods normal, 28-week morphology and growth normal, GBS swab pending at 36 weeks

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate counselling for planned mode of birth?

options.text: Recommend planned VBAC with continuous CTG monitoring in labour and counsel on a 0.5% uterine rupture risk, 70-75% success rate and the importance of intravenous access and group and hold on admission
options.rationale: One prior LSCS with no contraindications and a prior vaginal birth confers a 70-75% VBAC success rate; uterine rupture risk in spontaneous labour is approximately 0.5%. Continuous CTG, IV access and group and hold are standard. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean section)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean section

options.text: Recommend elective repeat caesarean section at 39 weeks because two prior births including one caesarean meet criteria that contraindicate VBAC at any Australian metropolitan maternity unit regardless of facility level
options.rationale: One prior LSCS is not a contraindication to VBAC and her prior vaginal birth is a strong predictor of success. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean section)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean section

options.text: Recommend induction of labour at 39 weeks with vaginal prostaglandin E2 and oxytocin augmentation as needed to achieve delivery before 40 weeks and minimise uterine rupture risk associated with post-dates pregnancy
options.rationale: Prostaglandin induction in VBAC is associated with a substantially increased uterine rupture risk (around 2-2.5%) and is generally avoided in Australian practice; mechanical induction is preferred if induction is needed. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean section)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean section

options.text: Recommend VBAC at home with a private midwife and continuous Doppler auscultation every 15 minutes in active labour because her prior vaginal birth makes the rupture risk negligible and hospital transfer is feasible
options.rationale: VBAC requires immediate access to emergency caesarean and on-site neonatal resuscitation; home VBAC is not endorsed by RANZCOG given the consequences of uterine rupture. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean section)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean section

options.text: Defer the decision until 38 weeks and reassess based on cervical length and Bishop score at that time, planning VBAC only if the cervix is favourable for spontaneous labour without any need for cervical ripening
options.rationale: Mode-of-birth decisions are made earlier so that planning and consent are documented; cervical examination at 38 weeks does not reliably predict VBAC success. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean section)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean section

explanation.summary: VBAC after one prior LSCS has a 60-80% success rate, with the strongest predictor being prior vaginal delivery. RANZCOG endorses VBAC in units with on-site theatre, anaesthesia, blood bank and neonatal resuscitation. Continuous CTG in labour, IV access and group and hold are standard. Uterine rupture risk in spontaneous labour is 0.2-0.7%; this rises to 1-1.5% with oxytocin augmentation and 2-2.5% with prostaglandin induction. Contraindications include prior classical or T-incision, prior uterine rupture, three or more prior caesareans and placenta praevia or accreta.
key_points: VBAC success after 1 LSCS: 60-80%, higher with prior vaginal birth
Rupture risk spontaneous labour: 0.2-0.7%; oxytocin 1-1.5%; PGE2 2-2.5%
Continuous CTG, IV access, group and hold are standard
Contraindications: classical/T-incision, prior rupture, 3+ prior LSCS, placenta praevia
Mechanical (Foley) preferred over prostaglandin for induction
explanation.pearls: Bandl's ring, sudden fetal heart deceleration with loss of station, scar tenderness and haematuria are textbook rupture signs but the most reliable in modern practice is a sudden non-reassuring CTG with maternal haemodynamic change - act on the CTG, not on waiting for classical signs.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean section
